_meta.note: All 19 Tier-1 states (CA, CO, CT, DE, IA, IN, KY, MD, MN, MT, NE, NH, NJ, OR, RI, TN, TX, UT, VA) are grounded directly in statute text on file as of 2026-07-12, including the schema's cra_notice_threshold field (added to distinguish consumer-reporting-agency notice duties from AG notice duties — several states have one but not the other, e.g. CO has a CRA duty but no numeric AG threshold while CT/CA/TN/KY/MN/IN have no CRA duty at all; do not assume any given duty by analogy to another state). CA/TX/VA's old general-knowledge placeholders have been replaced with text-grounded entries. RI's consumer_notice_required is flagged PARTIAL in its own entry — its notice-method provisions weren't found in the source section on file. All 50 states + DC have breach-notification source text available in the skill folder; Tier-2 (~30 states + DC) is unfilled, not unavailable — see BUILD_PLAN_PHASE6.md Phase 2 for the state list. Do not rely on any value below for actual client advice until marked verified.

CA.notification_deadline: Two different deadlines apply depending on entity type. For businesses/individuals (§1798.82), as amended by SB 446 effective January 1, 2026 (already in force): disclosure must be made within 30 calendar days of discovery or notification of the breach, subject to delay for legitimate law enforcement needs or as necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore reasonable system integrity. For state agencies (§1798.29, NOT amended by SB 446): disclosure must be made in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with the same law-enforcement/scope-determination exceptions — no fixed numeric day cap for agencies. A data holder that doesn't own the data must notify the owner/licensee immediately following discovery, for both agencies and businesses.
CA.ag_notice_threshold: Both sections require electronic submission of a single sample copy of the breach notification (with PII excluded) to the Attorney General if the breach requires notifying more than 500 California residents. For businesses (§1798.82(f)), this AG submission must be made within 15 calendar days of notifying affected consumers (added by SB 446, effective Jan. 1, 2026). For state agencies (§1798.29(e)), the statute requires the submission but does not specify an independent numeric deadline beyond the general notification-timing requirement.
CA.cra_notice_threshold: Neither §1798.29 nor §1798.82 contains any requirement to notify nationwide consumer reporting agencies. This is a genuine gap in California's statute, confirmed by a full read of both sections — do not assume a CRA duty by analogy to other states.
CA.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written; electronic (per 15 U.S.C. §7001); or substitute notice if the entity demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or it lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must include: (1) email notice where an email address is available, (2) conspicuous posting on the entity's website for a minimum of 30 days, and (3) notification to major statewide media (agencies must also notify the Office of Information Security within the Department of Technology). Notices must be titled 'Notice of Data Breach,' written in plain language, presented under specified headings ('What Happened,' 'What Information Was Involved,' 'What We Are Doing,' 'What You Can Do,' 'For More Information'), in type no smaller than 10-point, with detailed required content (breach date/date range, description, law-enforcement-delay disclosure, and credit-reporting-agency contact info if SSN/driver's license/CA ID was exposed). Special rule: if only online-account login credentials were breached, notice may instead direct the person to promptly change their password/security question; notice cannot be sent to the breached email account's own address. Identity-theft-mitigation mandate: businesses only (§1798.82(d)(2)(G)) — if the business itself was the source of the breach and the breach exposed SSN or driver's license/CA ID card number, the business must offer identity theft prevention and mitigation services at no cost for not less than 12 months. State agencies have no equivalent mitigation-service mandate. HIPAA-covered entities are deemed compliant with the content/format requirements if fully compliant with HITECH Act §13402(f).
CA.source_note: Extracted directly from ca-civ-code-1798.29-1798.82-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection or the prior placeholder entry. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use. Flag: the business-side 30-day deadline is from SB 446, effective Jan. 1, 2026 — older secondary sources describing pre-2026 CA law will be wrong on this point.

TX.notification_deadline: Disclosure to affected individuals must be made without unreasonable delay and in each case not later than the 60th day after the date the person determines the breach occurred, except for delay needed for law enforcement purposes or as necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore reasonable system integrity. A data holder that doesn't own the data must notify the owner/license holder immediately after discovering the breach. Special multistate provision: if the affected individual resides in a state with its own breach notice law, the notice may instead be given under that state's law.
TX.ag_notice_threshold: If the breach involves at least 250 Texas residents, the person must notify the Texas Attorney General as soon as practicable and not later than the 30th day after the date the person determines the breach occurred. Notice must be submitted electronically via a form on the AG's website and must include: a detailed description of the breach/misuse, the number of Texas residents affected, the number already notified by mail or other direct method, measures taken and planned, and whether law enforcement is investigating. The AG posts a public listing of received notifications (excluding sensitive personal information), updated within 30 days of each new notification, with entries removed after one year absent a further breach notification.
TX.cra_notice_threshold: If the person is required to notify more than 10,000 persons at one time, it must also notify each nationwide consumer reporting agency (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices, without unreasonable delay.
TX.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be given by: (1) written notice at the individual's last known address; (2) electronic notice, if provided in accordance with 15 U.S.C. §7001; or (3) substitute notice if the person demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $250,000, the number of affected persons exceeds 500,000, or it lacks sufficient contact information — substitute notice consists of email (if addresses are available), conspicuous website posting, and notice published in or broadcast on major statewide media. A person with its own compliant information-security-policy notification procedure is deemed compliant if it follows that policy. No identity-theft-mitigation-service mandate anywhere in the statute.
TX.source_note: Extracted directly from tx-bc-521.053-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection or the prior placeholder entry. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

VA.notification_deadline: Notification is owed only where unauthorized access/acquisition of unencrypted, unredacted personal information causes, or is reasonably believed will cause, identity theft or other fraud to a Virginia resident (a harm-triggered standard, not mere unauthorized access). Where owed, disclosure to both the Office of the Attorney General and affected residents must be made without unreasonable delay following discovery or notification of the breach, subject to reasonable delay to determine scope/restore integrity and further delay if law enforcement advises that notice would impede an investigation or national/homeland security. A data holder that doesn't own the data must notify the owner/licensee without unreasonable delay following discovery.
VA.ag_notice_threshold: No numeric resident threshold — the Office of the Attorney General must be notified at the same time and under the same trigger as any notice to affected Virginia residents. Separately, employers/payroll service providers must notify the AG without unreasonable delay of any breach compromising income-tax-withholding data (taxpayer ID + withheld income tax), regardless of resident count, with content limited to the employer's name and federal EIN; the AG relays this to the Department of Taxation.
VA.cra_notice_threshold: If notice is given to more than 1,000 persons at one time, the entity must also notify, without unreasonable delay, the Office of the Attorney General (again, specifically) and all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice.
VA.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be: (1) written notice to the last known postal address; (2) telephone notice; (3) electronic notice; or (4) substitute notice if the entity demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $50,000, the affected class of Virginia residents exceeds 100,000, or it lacks sufficient contact information or consent to use methods 1-3. Substitute notice must include: (a) email notice if email addresses are available, (b) conspicuous website posting, and (c) notice to major statewide media. Required notice content (all methods): a general description of the incident, the type of personal information involved, general protective measures taken, a phone number for further information (if one exists), and advice to review account statements and monitor free credit reports. No mandated free credit-monitoring/identity-theft-mitigation service — the statute only requires advising residents to monitor their own free credit reports. Notable exemptions: GLBA Title V entities with compliant procedures, entities following their primary regulator's breach rules, and State Corporation Commission Bureau of Insurance-regulated entities are exempt. AG enforcement carries a civil penalty of up to $150,000 per breach, without limiting individuals' ability to recover direct economic damages.
VA.source_note: Extracted directly from va-code-18.2-186.6-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection or the prior placeholder entry. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

CO.notification_deadline: Most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, but not later than 30 days after the date of determination that a security breach occurred.
CO.ag_notice_threshold: Notice to the Colorado Attorney General required within the same 30-day window if the breach is reasonably believed to have affected 500 or more Colorado residents.
CO.cra_notice_threshold: Notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined by the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act, 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) required, in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, if the covered entity must notify more than 1,000 Colorado residents. Must state the anticipated date of resident notification and the approximate number of residents to be notified; does not require disclosing recipients' names or other personal information. Does not apply to covered entities subject to Title V of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act.
CO.consumer_notice_required: Written, telephonic, or electronic notice to affected residents; substitute notice (email + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide media) available only if notice cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 250,000 Colorado residents, or the covered entity lacks sufficient contact information.
CO.source_note: Extracted directly from the full C.R.S. Title 6 text in the skill folder (crs2024-title-06.txt, §6-1-716) — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use, per _meta.

CT.notification_deadline: Without unreasonable delay, but not later than 60 days after discovery of the breach, unless a shorter time is required under federal law (subject to delay for an active law-enforcement investigation, per subsec. (d)). If additional affected residents are identified after the 60-day window has passed, the entity must proceed in good faith to notify those additional residents as expediently as possible. Notification is not required if, after appropriate investigation, the entity reasonably determines the breach is not likely to result in harm to the affected individuals.
CT.ag_notice_threshold: Notice to the Connecticut Attorney General required not later than the time when notice is provided to residents (i.e., whenever any Connecticut resident must be notified — no separate numeric floor).
CT.cra_notice_threshold: Not required. Conn. Gen. Stat. §36a-701b contains no consumer-reporting-agency notification provision (unlike Colorado's statute) — confirmed by full-text review, no such requirement appears anywhere in the section.
CT.consumer_notice_required: Written notice, telephone notice, or electronic notice (provided the electronic notice is consistent with the federal E-SIGN Act's electronic records/signatures provisions, 15 U.S.C. §7001). Substitute notice (email if addresses known, conspicuous website posting, and notice to major statewide media) is permitted if the entity demonstrates that the cost of providing notice would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or the entity does not have sufficient contact information. For breaches involving Social Security or taxpayer ID numbers, the entity must also offer appropriate identity-theft prevention and mitigation services (e.g., credit monitoring) at no cost for at least 24 months, and provide information on how to enroll.
CT.source_note: Extracted directly from the Connecticut breach notification statute text in the skill folder (ct-gen-stat-36a-701b-breach-notification.txt) — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use, per _meta.

DE.statute_citation: Del. Code Ann. tit. 6, Ch. 12B, §§ 12B-100 through 12B-104
DE.notification_deadline: Notice to affected residents must be made without unreasonable delay but not later than 60 days after determination of the breach of security. Exceptions: (1) a shorter time is required under federal law; (2) a law-enforcement agency may request delay if notice would impede a criminal investigation, with notice then required after the agency determines notice will not compromise the investigation; (3) if the person could not, through reasonable diligence, identify within 60 days that certain residents' personal information was included in the breach, notice to those residents must be given as soon as practicable after that later determination, unless substitute notice has already been provided.
DE.ag_notice_threshold: AG notice is required only if the affected number of Delaware residents to be notified exceeds 500. When triggered, notice to the Attorney General must be provided no later than the time notice is provided to residents.
DE.cra_notice_threshold: The statute contains no provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies at any threshold.
DE.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, telephonic, or electronic (electronic notice is permitted if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001 or if the person's primary means of communication with the resident is electronic). Substitute notice is permitted if the person demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $75,000, the affected class exceeds 100,000 Delaware residents, or the person lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice must include: (1) email notice if email addresses are available, (2) conspicuous posting on the person's website (if any), and (3) notice to major statewide media (newspapers, radio, TV) and publication on major social media platforms. If the breach includes a Social Security number, the person must offer affected residents free credit monitoring services for 1 year, including information on how to enroll and how to place a credit freeze; this mitigation-service mandate does not apply if, after appropriate investigation, the person reasonably determines the breach is unlikely to result in harm. Special rule for breached email-account login credentials: notice cannot be sent to the compromised email address itself; another method must be used, or clear and conspicuous online notice delivered when the resident is connected to the account.
DE.source_note: Extracted directly from de-code-title6-ch12B-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

IA.notification_deadline: Notification to affected consumers must be made 'in the most expeditious manner possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and with measures necessary to determine contact information, the scope of the breach, and to restore data integrity/security. The statute sets no fixed numeric outer-day cap. Notification may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it will impede a criminal investigation and makes a written request; notice must then follow after the agency determines it will not compromise the investigation and notifies the person in writing.
IA.ag_notice_threshold: AG notice (to the director of the consumer protection division of the Iowa Attorney General's office) is required only where the breach requires notification to more than 500 Iowa residents. When triggered, written notice to the AG's office must be given within 5 business days after notice is given to any consumer.
IA.cra_notice_threshold: The statute contains no provision requiring direct notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies at any threshold. It only requires that the consumer notice itself include contact information for consumer reporting agencies as part of its content.
IA.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be given by: (a) written notice to the last available address on record; (b) electronic notice if the person's customary method of communication with the consumer is electronic, or is consistent with Iowa Ch. 554D and the federal E-SIGN Act (15 U.S.C. §7001); or (c) substitute notice if the person demonstrates cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 350,000 persons, or the person lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must consist of: (1) email notice where email addresses are available, (2) conspicuous posting (or a link) on the person's website, and (3) notification to major statewide media. No identity-theft-mitigation-service (e.g., credit monitoring) mandate exists in this statute. Notable exemptions: entities complying with a primary/functional federal regulator's breach rules providing equal or greater protection, entities complying with other state/federal law providing equal or greater protection, and entities subject to and compliant with GLBA Title V or HIPAA/HITECH.
IA.source_note: Extracted directly from ia-code-ch715c-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

IN.notification_deadline: Disclosure/notification must be made without unreasonable delay, but not more than 45 days after discovery of the breach (this 45-day outer limit was added effective July 1, 2022; the statute previously only had the 'without unreasonable delay' standard with no fixed cap). A delay is considered reasonable if necessary to restore system integrity, necessary to discover the scope of the breach, or made in response to a request from the Attorney General or a law enforcement agency because disclosure would impede a criminal/civil investigation or jeopardize national security. Once the reason for delay ends, disclosure must be made as soon as possible.
IN.ag_notice_threshold: The statute contains NO requirement to notify the Indiana Attorney General of a breach at any threshold. The Attorney General's only role under this statute is enforcement (bringing actions for violations), not receiving breach notice.
IN.cra_notice_threshold: If disclosure is required to more than 1,000 consumers, the data base owner must also disclose to each nationwide consumer reporting agency (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) information necessary to assist the agency in preventing fraud, including affected residents' personal information.
IN.consumer_notice_required: Disclosure must be made using one of: mail, telephone, facsimile (fax), or electronic mail (only if the data base owner has the resident's email address on file). If disclosure is required to more than 500,000 Indiana residents, or if the data base owner determines the cost of disclosure will exceed $250,000, the owner may elect to use both: (1) conspicuous posting on the data base owner's website (if any), and (2) notice to major news reporting media in the geographic area where affected residents reside (this is an election available in addition to, not strictly a substitute requiring, insufficient-contact-info as a trigger — the statute conditions it on resident-count or cost, not lack of contact information). No identity-theft-mitigation-service (credit monitoring) mandate exists in this statute. Notable exemptions: entities maintaining their own disclosure procedures under specified federal regimes (USA PATRIOT Act, Executive Order 13224, Driver's Privacy Protection Act, FCRA, GLBA, HIPAA) that provide breach notice without unreasonable delay per those policies are not required to make a separate disclosure; financial institutions complying with the federal Interagency Guidance on Response Programs are also exempt from a separate disclosure. By its own terms, this article does not apply to state agencies or the judicial/legislative branches.
IN.source_note: Extracted directly from in-code-24-4.9-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

KY.notification_deadline: Disclosure to affected Kentucky residents must be made 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and any measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore data integrity. No fixed numeric outer-day cap is stated. Notification may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it would impede a criminal investigation; notice must then be made promptly after the agency determines it will not compromise the investigation.
KY.ag_notice_threshold: The statute contains NO requirement to notify the Kentucky Attorney General at any threshold — flagged as a notable gap relative to most other states.
KY.cra_notice_threshold: If circumstances require notification of more than 1,000 persons at one time, the person must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies and credit bureaus (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices.
KY.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be provided by: (a) written notice; (b) electronic notice, if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001; or (c) substitute notice, if the information holder demonstrates cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or it lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must consist of: (1) email notice where email addresses are available, (2) conspicuous posting on the information holder's website (if any), and (3) notification to major statewide media. No identity-theft-mitigation-service (credit monitoring) mandate exists in this statute. Notable exemptions: the statute (and related nonaffiliated-third-party requirements in KRS Ch. 61) does not apply to persons subject to GLBA Title V, HIPAA, or to Commonwealth agencies/local governments/political subdivisions.
KY.source_note: Extracted directly from ky-rev-stat-365.732-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MD.statute_citation: Md. Code Ann., Com. Law §§ 14-3501 through 14-3508
MD.notification_deadline: Notice to affected individuals must be given as soon as reasonably practicable, but not later than 45 days after the business discovers or is notified of the breach. (Separately, if the entity holding the data does not own/license it, it must notify the owner/licensee as soon as reasonably practicable but not later than 10 days after discovery.) Notification may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it would impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize homeland/national security, or to determine the scope of the breach, identify affected individuals, or restore system integrity. If delayed due to a law-enforcement request, notice must then be given not later than: 7 days after law enforcement determines it will no longer impede the investigation/jeopardize security, if the original 45-day period has already elapsed; or the end of the original 45-day period, if it has not yet elapsed.
MD.ag_notice_threshold: There is no numeric resident threshold — AG notice is required for ANY breach that triggers individual notification. Distinctively, Maryland requires notice to the Office of the Attorney General BEFORE notice is given to affected individuals — the reverse sequencing of most states. The AG notice must include the number of affected Maryland residents, a description of the breach, steps taken/planned, and the form and a sample of the notice to be sent to individuals.
MD.cra_notice_threshold: If a business is required to give breach notice to 1,000 or more individuals, it must also notify, without unreasonable delay, each nationwide consumer reporting agency (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices. This need not include recipients' names or other personal identifying information.
MD.consumer_notice_required: Notice must be given by: written notice to the individual's most recent address on file; electronic mail, if the individual expressly consented to electronic notice or the business conducts its business primarily through Internet transactions; telephonic notice; or substitute notice if the business lacks sufficient contact information for the other methods. Substitute notice must consist of: (1) email notice where an email address is available, (2) conspicuous posting on the business's website (if any), and (3) notification to major print or broadcast media in the geographic areas where affected individuals likely reside. No general identity-theft-mitigation-service (credit monitoring) mandate exists in this statute. Special rule: for a breach involving only email-account access credentials (username/email + password or security Q&A) and no other personal information, the business may comply by directing the individual to change their password/security question or take other protective steps, delivered by any method except email to the breached account itself. Notable exemptions: businesses complying with GLBA §501(b)/FACTA §216/federal Interagency Guidelines and Guidance, or complying with HIPAA, are deemed compliant with this subtitle, as are their affiliates.
MD.source_note: Extracted directly from md-com-law-14-3501-3508-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MN.notification_deadline: Disclosure to affected residents must be made 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and with measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach, identify affected individuals, and restore data system integrity. No fixed numeric outer-day cap is stated. Notification may be delayed to a date certain if a law enforcement agency affirmatively determines that notification will impede a criminal investigation.
MN.ag_notice_threshold: The statute contains NO requirement to notify the Minnesota Attorney General of a breach at any threshold; the Attorney General's role under this statute is limited to enforcement under Minn. Stat. §8.31.
MN.cra_notice_threshold: If circumstances requiring notification affect more than 500 persons at one time, the person must also notify all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices, within 48 hours.
MN.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be provided by: (1) written notice to the most recent address on file; (2) electronic notice, if the person's primary method of communication with the individual is electronic, or if the notice is consistent with the E-SIGN Act (15 U.S.C. §7001); or (3) substitute notice, if the person demonstrates cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or the person lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must consist of all of: (i) email notice where an email address is available, (ii) conspicuous posting on the person's website (if any), and (iii) notification to major statewide media. No identity-theft-mitigation-service (credit monitoring) mandate exists in this statute. Notable exemption: the statute does not apply at all to 'financial institutions' as defined by 15 U.S.C. §6809(3).
MN.source_note: Extracted directly from mn-stat-325e.61-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MT.notification_deadline: No numeric day cap. Disclosure to affected Montana residents must be made 'without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and with measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. Notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines notice would impede a criminal investigation and requests a delay; notice must then be made once law enforcement determines it will not compromise the investigation. A third party that merely maintains (does not own) the data must notify the data's owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery.'
MT.ag_notice_threshold: No numeric resident threshold. Any person or business required to issue notification under this section must simultaneously submit an electronic copy of the notification (plus date/method of distribution) to the Attorney General's consumer protection office whenever ANY individual notification is required — personally identifying information about recipients is excluded, and if notice goes to more than one individual only a single copy indicating the number of in-state recipients is required.
MT.cra_notice_threshold: The statute does NOT impose a general mandatory duty to notify nationwide consumer reporting agencies of the breach itself, and there is no numeric threshold for such notice. It contains only a narrower, conditional duty: if the business's notice to an individual suggests, indicates, or implies that the individual may obtain a copy of their file from a consumer credit reporting agency, the business must coordinate with that CRA on the timing, content, and distribution of the notice (without unreasonably delaying notice to the individual).
MT.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), telephonic, or substitute notice if the business demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or the business lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must consist of: email notice (if email addresses are available) AND conspicuous posting on the business's website (if it maintains one) AND notification to applicable local or statewide media. A business with its own information-security-policy notification procedures that doesn't unreasonably delay notice is deemed compliant if it follows those procedures. No statutory mandate for free credit monitoring or identity-theft mitigation services.
MT.source_note: Extracted directly from mt-code-ann-30-14-1701-1705-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NE.notification_deadline: No numeric day cap. Notice to affected Nebraska residents must be made 'as soon as possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and with measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the computerized data system. Notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it will impede a criminal investigation; notice must then be made in good faith, without unreasonable delay, as soon as possible after law enforcement determines notification will no longer impede the investigation.
NE.ag_notice_threshold: No numeric resident threshold. Whenever notice to a Nebraska resident is required, the entity must also provide notice of the breach to the Attorney General no later than the time notice is provided to the resident.
NE.cra_notice_threshold: This statute contains no provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies at any threshold — flagged in the source file itself as an unusual gap worth double-checking against current Nebraska AG guidance.
NE.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, telephonic, electronic (if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001 as it existed 1/1/2006), or substitute notice under one of two regimes: (1) general — if cost would exceed $75,000, the affected class of Nebraska residents exceeds 100,000, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information, substitute notice requires email (if addresses available) + conspicuous website posting (if a site is maintained) + notice to major statewide media outlets; or (2) small-business — for entities with 10 or fewer employees demonstrating cost would exceed $10,000, substitute notice requires email (if available) + a paid local newspaper ad covering at least one-quarter page, published weekly for 3 consecutive weeks + conspicuous website posting (if maintained) + notice to major local media outlets. An entity following its own information-security-policy notice procedures (consistent with the statute's timing) is deemed compliant, as is an entity following procedures set by its primary/functional state or federal regulator. No statutory mandate for free credit monitoring or identity-theft mitigation services.
NE.source_note: Extracted directly from ne-rev-stat-87-801-808-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NH.notification_deadline: No numeric day cap. Once a person determines misuse has occurred or is reasonably likely (or cannot make a determination), disclosure to affected individuals must be made 'as quickly as possible' after that determination. Notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency, or a national or homeland security agency, determines notification would impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize national/homeland security.
NH.ag_notice_threshold: No numeric resident threshold — notice is required whenever notice to individuals is required. However, WHO receives that notice depends on the entity: any person engaged in trade or commerce subject to RSA 358-A:3, I must notify their primary regulator instead of the AG; all other persons must notify the New Hampshire Attorney General's office. The notice to the regulator/AG must include the anticipated date of notice to individuals and the approximate number of New Hampshire individuals to be notified (no names or personal information required).
NH.cra_notice_threshold: Numeric threshold of 1,000 consumers. If more than 1,000 consumers must be notified, the person must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the anticipated notification date, the approximate number of consumers to be notified, and the content of the notice. This CRA-notice duty does NOT apply to a person subject to Title V of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act.
NH.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written; electronic — but ONLY if the business's primary means of communication with affected individuals is by electronic means; telephonic — provided a log of each notification is kept; or substitute notice if cost would exceed $5,000, the affected class exceeds 1,000 individuals, or the person lacks sufficient contact information or consent to use the other methods. Substitute notice must include: email notice (if addresses available) + conspicuous posting on the business's website (if maintained) + notification to major statewide media. A person may also comply via its own internal notification procedures maintained as part of an information security policy. Entities subject to RSA 358-A:3, I that follow their regulator's breach-notification rules/guidance are deemed compliant. No statutory mandate for free credit monitoring or identity-theft mitigation services.
NH.source_note: Extracted directly from nh-rsa-359-c19-21-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NJ.notification_deadline: No numeric day cap. Disclosure to affected New Jersey customers must be made 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement and with measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. Disclosure is NOT required at all if the business/public entity establishes in writing (retained 5 years) that misuse of the information is not reasonably possible. Notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines notice would impede a criminal or civil investigation and requests the delay; notice must then be made once law enforcement determines disclosure will not compromise the investigation. A third party that merely maintains (does not own) the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery.'
NJ.ag_notice_threshold: This statute contains NO Attorney General notice requirement at any threshold. Instead, it requires that ANY business or public entity required to disclose a breach must, in advance of notifying the customer (i.e., regardless of the number of residents affected — no threshold), report the breach and related information to the Division of State Police in the Department of Law and Public Safety for investigation/handling. This is a law-enforcement reporting duty, not an AG consumer-protection notice, and should not be conflated with an AG-notice requirement.
NJ.cra_notice_threshold: Numeric threshold of 1,000 persons notified at one time. If circumstances require notifying more than 1,000 persons at one time, the business/public entity must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices.
NJ.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written or electronic (if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if the entity demonstrates cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or it lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must consist of: email notice (if an email address is available) + conspicuous posting on the entity's website (if maintained) + notification to major statewide media. An entity following its own information-security-policy notice procedures (consistent with the statute's requirements) is deemed compliant. Special 2019 carve-out: for a breach involving only a username/email or password/security Q&A (no other statutory personal information), the entity may instead provide notice directing the customer to promptly change their password/security question and to protect other accounts using the same credentials; notice may NOT be sent to an email account that is itself the subject of the breach — an alternate method or conspicuous online notice (when the customer connects from a known/customary access point) must be used instead. No statutory mandate for free credit monitoring or identity-theft mitigation services.
NJ.source_note: Extracted directly from nj-stat-56.8-163-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

OR.notification_deadline: Notice must be given in the most expeditious manner possible, without unreasonable delay, but no later than 45 days after discovering or receiving notification of the breach. Before giving notice, the covered entity must undertake reasonable measures to determine sufficient contact information for recipients, determine the scope of the breach, and restore the reasonable integrity, security, and confidentiality of the personal information. Notice may be delayed only if a law enforcement agency determines notification would impede a criminal investigation and requests the delay in writing. Separately, a vendor (service provider) must notify the covered entity with which it contracts as soon as practicable but no later than 10 days after discovering the breach or having reason to believe one occurred (a pipeline deadline, not the consumer-facing one).
OR.ag_notice_threshold: Numeric threshold of 250 consumers. The covered entity must notify the Attorney General (in writing or electronically) if the number of consumers to be notified exceeds 250. A vendor must independently notify the AG if its own breach involved personal information of more than 250 consumers (or an indeterminable number), unless the covered entity has already notified the AG. Notably, even entities otherwise exempt from the consumer-notice requirements must still provide the AG a copy of any notice sent to consumers or to their primary/functional regulator, within a reasonable time, if the breach affects more than 250 consumers.
OR.cra_notice_threshold: Numeric threshold of 1,000 consumers. If a breach affects more than 1,000 consumers, the covered entity must notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies of the timing, distribution, and content of the consumer notice, and must include any police report number assigned to the breach. The covered entity may NOT delay notifying affected consumers in order to notify the CRAs.
OR.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be given in writing; electronically — only if the covered entity customarily communicates with the consumer electronically, or if consistent with the E-SIGN Act (15 U.S.C. §7001) as it existed 1/1/2020; by telephone if the entity contacts the consumer directly; or via substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 350,000 consumers, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice consists of: conspicuous posting of the notice (or a link) on the entity's website (if maintained) + notifying major statewide television and newspaper media. Exemptions from the notice requirements exist for: entities complying with their primary/functional federal regulator's own breach notification rules; entities complying with other state/federal law providing equal-or-greater protection; entities complying with GLBA Title V regulations; and entities complying with HIPAA/HITECH — but exempt entities affected by breaches over 250 consumers must still send the AG a notice copy. If the covered entity (or its agent/affiliate) voluntarily offers free credit monitoring or identity theft prevention/mitigation services in connection with the notice, it may not condition that offer on the consumer providing a credit/debit card number or accepting a separate fee-based service, and must clearly disclose any fee if additional paid services are offered — but the statute does not itself mandate that free credit monitoring be offered.
OR.source_note: Extracted directly from or-rev-stat-646a.604-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

RI.notification_deadline: Notification must be made in the most expedient time possible, subject to differing caps by entity type, both measured from confirmation of the breach and the ability to ascertain the information required for the notice content (not from mere discovery): for state and municipal agencies, no later than 30 calendar days; for all other persons/businesses, no later than 45 calendar days. Notice may be delayed if a federal, state, or local law enforcement agency determines notification would impede a criminal investigation; that agency must notify the entity of the delay request without unreasonable delay, and once law enforcement clears the delay, notice must be provided as soon as practicable under the applicable 30/45-day framework. Where affected employees are represented by a union, the employer must also notify the collective bargaining agent.
RI.ag_notice_threshold: Numeric threshold of 500 Rhode Island residents (applies identically to both state/municipal agencies and private persons/businesses). If more than 500 Rhode Island residents are to be notified, the entity must notify the Attorney General as to the timing, content, and distribution of the notices and the approximate number of affected individuals. This AG notice must not delay notice to affected residents.
RI.cra_notice_threshold: Same numeric threshold as the AG trigger — 500 Rhode Island residents. If more than 500 residents are to be notified, the entity must simultaneously notify the major credit reporting agencies as to the timing, content, and distribution of the notices and the approximate number of affected individuals, without delaying notice to residents.
RI.consumer_notice_required: PARTIAL — this particular source section (§11-49.3-4) does not itself specify the acceptable methods of notice to individuals (e.g., written/electronic/telephonic) or a substitute-notice mechanism/cost threshold — that provision likely resides in an adjacent section of the Identity Theft Protection Act not captured in this source file; flag for follow-up before relying on this for a substitute-notice analysis. What this section does require: the notice to individuals must include (to the extent known) a general/brief description of the incident and how it occurred, the number of affected individuals, the type of information breached, the date/estimated date/date range of the breach, the date the breach was discovered, a clear description of any remediation services offered (including contact info for credit reporting agencies, remediation service providers, and the Attorney General), and a description of the consumer's ability to file a police report and how to request a security freeze (noting CRA fees may apply). Identity-theft-mitigation-service duration mandate applies only to state and municipal agencies — their remediation services must include a minimum of 5 years of coverage for individuals 18 and older, and for individuals under 18, coverage until age 18 plus no less than 2 additional years beyond turning 18. No equivalent mandatory minimum service or duration is imposed on private persons/businesses.
RI.source_note: Extracted directly from ri-gen-laws-11-49.3-4-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use. NOTE: consumer_notice_required is flagged PARTIAL — the notice-method/substitute-notice provisions weren't found in this section and may live in an adjacent section of the same act not yet in the skill folder.

TN.notification_deadline: Notice to affected Tennessee residents must be made no later than 45 days from discovery or notification of the breach of system security, unless a longer period is required due to the legitimate needs of law enforcement — in which case notice must be made no later than 45 days after law enforcement determines that notification will not compromise the investigation. A third-party data holder (an information holder that maintains but does not own the data) must notify the owner/licensee of the information on the same 45-day timeline (subject to the same law enforcement delay).
TN.ag_notice_threshold: No Attorney General notification requirement exists anywhere in this statute, at any threshold — a notable gap for Tennessee, confirmed on a full read of the text.
TN.cra_notice_threshold: If the breach requires notification of more than 1,000 persons at one time, the information holder must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies and credit bureaus (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices.
TN.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written; electronic (if consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001, or if the information holder's primary method of communication with the resident is already electronic); or substitute notice if the information holder demonstrates the cost of notice would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or it lacks sufficient contact information. Substitute notice must include: (1) email notice where an email address is available, (2) conspicuous posting on the information holder's website (if it maintains one), and (3) notification to major statewide media. An information holder with its own compliant information-security-policy notification procedure is deemed compliant if it follows that policy. No identity-theft-mitigation-service (e.g., free credit monitoring) mandate anywhere in the statute. Notable exemptions: entities subject to GLBA Title V or HIPAA/HITECH are exempt from this section entirely. The statute also creates a private right of action for injured customers.
TN.source_note: Extracted directly from tn-code-ann-47-18-2107-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

UT.notification_deadline: Notification is owed only where an investigation reveals that misuse of personal information for identity theft or fraud has occurred or is reasonably likely to occur (a harm-triggered standard, not mere unauthorized access). Where owed, notice must be provided in the most expedient time possible without unreasonable delay, considering legitimate law enforcement investigative needs, after determining the scope of the breach, and after restoring the reasonable integrity of the system. Notice may be delayed at the request of a law enforcement agency that determines it would impede a criminal investigation; once law enforcement clears it, notice must be given in good faith without unreasonable delay in the most expedient time possible. A data holder that doesn't own the data must notify and cooperate with the owner/licensee immediately following discovery of the breach, if misuse has occurred or is reasonably likely to occur.
UT.ag_notice_threshold: If the investigation reveals misuse (or reasonably likely misuse) affecting 500 or more Utah residents, notice must also be given to the Office of the Attorney General and to the Utah Cyber Center. Below 500 residents, no AG/Cyber Center notice is required.
UT.cra_notice_threshold: If the investigation reveals misuse (or reasonably likely misuse) affecting 1,000 or more Utah residents, notice must also be given to each nationwide consumer reporting agency (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a).
UT.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be given: (i) in writing by first-class mail to the resident's most recent known address; (ii) electronically, if the person's primary method of communication with the resident is electronic, or per 15 U.S.C. §7001; (iii) by telephone, including automatic dialing technology not otherwise prohibited by law; or (iv) for residents for whom none of the above is feasible, by publishing notice in a newspaper of general circulation and as required under Utah Code §45-1-101. Unlike several other states, Utah's statute does not set a dollar-cost or affected-class-size threshold for substitute/published notice — it turns instead on feasibility of the other methods. A person with its own compliant information-security-policy notification procedure is deemed compliant, as is a person regulated by a state/federal regulator who follows that regulator's applicable breach procedures. No identity-theft-mitigation-service mandate in the statute. Notification content to the AG/Cyber Center (when required) must include breach date, discovery date, total number affected (including Utah residents), types of personal information involved, and a short description of the breach; that submission and related work product may be classified as a protected record. A waiver of the statute's protections is void and unenforceable.
